sections.summary: This standard applies to the fresh rhizome of commercial varieties of ginger (Zingiber officinale Roscoe) supplied to the consumer after preparation and packaging and excludes ginger intended for industrial processing.

Sets out quality provisions for ginger including minimum requirements, suitability for transport, and a three-class classification system (Extra, Class I, Class II) with associated allowable defects and tolerances.

Specifies minimum quality requirements for ginger: whole, sound, clean, practically free of visible foreign matter and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odours/tastes, firm, sufficiently dried including harvest cuts, and able to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.

sections.section: 2.1.1  The development and condition of the ginger must be such as to enable it:
sections.summary: The ginger's development and condition must enable it to withstand transport and handling and to arrive in satisfactory condition at the place of destination.

Classifies ginger into three classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with Extra requiring superior, well‑shaped, clean roots with only very slight superficial defects; Class I requiring good, firm roots with limited slight defects (e.g., slight skin defects up to 10% surface area); and Class II for produce meeting the minimum requirements but allowing more and specified defects.

'Extra' Class ginger must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type, cleaned and well shaped, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I ginger must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type, with roots firm and showing no shrivelling, dehydration or sprouting, while slight defects are allowed provided they do not affect appearance, keeping quality or presentation (e.g., slight healed/dry skin defects up to 10% surface area).

Class I ginger must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type, with roots firm and showing no shrivelling, dehydration or sprouting, while slight defects are allowed provided they do not affect appearance, keeping quality or presentation (e.g., slight healed/dry skin defects up to 10% surface area).

Class II includes ginger that meets the minimum requirements but not higher classes; roots should be reasonably firm and specified defects are permitted such as healed/dry skin defects up to 15% surface area, early sprouting (≤10% by weight per unit), slight pest markings, healed dry cracks, slight traces of soil and bruises.
Size is determined by weight and the standard defines size codes with corresponding weights (e.g., Size A = 300 g, B = 200 g, C = 150 g).

sections.section: Size is determined by the weight of the ginger.
sections.summary: Size of ginger is determined by its weight.

Allows tolerances by package for produce not meeting class or size requirements; quality tolerances are: Extra 5% (may be Class I), Class I 10% (may be Class II), Class II 10% (may fail requirements except rotten/unfit produce), and size tolerances are 5% for Extra and 10% for Class I and II by number or weight.

Specifies presentation requirements: packages must be uniform (same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size) with visible contents representative of the whole and the heaviest rhizome ≤ twice the lightest; packaging must protect produce, use new/clean/non‑damaging materials and containers meeting hygiene, ventilation and resistance criteria, and comply with CAC/RCP 44-1995.

Each package must contain uniform ginger of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size with the visible portion representative of the whole, and the heaviest rhizome may not exceed twice the weight of the lightest.

Ginger must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean materials that do not cause damage and may include printed materials only with non‑toxic ink/glue, and packing must comply with the Recommended International Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CAC/RCP 44-1995).

Requires containers for ginger to meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength/resistance characteristics to ensure suitable handling, shipping and preservation, and to be free of foreign matter and odour.

Sets out labelling requirements in addition to Codex STAN 1-1985 for consumer and non-retail packages, specifying that consumer packages and non-retail containers must meet particular labelling provisions (see subsections) including identification, nature and origin of produce, commercial identification and an optional official inspection mark.

Introductory provision stating that consumer package labelling must comply with the Codex General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CODEX STAN 1-1985) plus the specific provisions that follow.
Requires consumer packages whose contents are not visible to be labelled with the name of the produce and optionally the variety and/or commercial type.
Requires non-retail containers to bear specified particulars grouped on the same side, legibly and indelibly marked and visible from the outside or provided in accompanying shipment documents.
Requires non-retail containers to show the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an identification code permitted as optional.
Requires non-retail containers to indicate the name of the produce if contents are not visible, with the variety and/or commercial type allowed as optional.

Requires declaration of the country of origin and optionally district or local place name; notes that origin may include recycled food‑grade material and that where code marks are used national law may require a close reference to the packer/dispatcher identification.
Specifies commercial identification requirements for non-retail containers: class and size (size code or minimum/maximum weight in grams) and optionally number of units and net weight.
Heading indicating that an official inspection mark may be applied; no substantive detail provided here.
Requires the produce to comply with Codex maximum levels for contaminants and toxins (CODEX STAN 193-1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends that produce be prepared and handled in accordance with relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice (e.g., CAC/RCP 1-1969, CAC/RCP 53-2003) and comply with any microbiological criteria established under CAC/GL 21-1997.
